description:
bl-f8f3 surfaced that bl-05e5's moved-clone test passed against broken code because the fixture re-wrote the breadcrumb post-rename to match the buggy impl's expectation. Both sides shared the same wrong mental model — `conformance_moved_clone.rs` did not exercise the real-world scenario it was named after.

That's a class-of-bug risk, not a one-off. The other surfaces bl-05e5 delivered (`bl prime --migrate`, the legacy-layout doctor finding, `bl repair --rebind-path`) have conformance tests written in the same sitting. If any of them was written by reading the impl rather than the SPEC scenario, they could be quietly tautological.

## Scope

Re-read every conformance test added by bl-05e5 against the SPEC scenario it claims to cover, asking specifically: does the fixture set up the state a *real user* would arrive in, or does it set up the state the *impl happens to handle*?

Concrete candidates to audit:

- `tests/conformance_moved_clone.rs` — already fixed in bl-f8f3, use as the worked example.
- Any conformance test touching `bl prime --migrate` — does the fixture model a clone that has never seen XDG (i.e. legacy `.balls/state-repo/` present, no XDG state), or does it pre-seed XDG state and just call migrate?
- The legacy-layout doctor finding — does the fixture stand up a legacy clone and assert doctor *flags* it, or does it pre-flag it?
- `bl repair --rebind-path` integration — covered by the bl-f8f3 fix transitively (third subtest), but spot-check.

For each, the audit either (a) confirms the fixture is faithful, or (b) files a follow-up bug ball with the inverted-predicate-style fix.

## Why not include in bl-f8f3

bl-f8f3 was a focused single-comparison bug. Folding an audit in would have blurred the diff. Audit is broader and may surface zero, one, or several follow-ups — best as its own bounded read pass.
title: Audit bl-05e5 conformance fixtures for impl/fixture co-authored assumptions
